Fern Por.rrrcer. PRACTTcEs CouvrrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-s660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
August 2,2007
VIA FACSIMILE AND U.S. MAIL
Tom McCabe
City of Encinitas Planning Commissioner
1701 Mackinnon Avenue
Cardiff by-the-Sea, California 92007
RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our FiIe No.I-07-111
Dear Mr. McCabe:
This letter is_a follow-up to previous advice regarding the Cardiff-by-the-Sea
Draft Specific Plan ("Draft Specific Plan') and youl d.rti"r under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act-(the "Acf'). I lsee McCabeAdvice Letter,
No' I-07-072.) Becatse your request does not involve specific decisions, butlefers to a
series of broad topic areas for discussions about the Drait Specific plan, we are providing
you with general guidelines that may be applied. Therefore, we treat your le6er as a
request for informal assistance.2
Please note that our advice is based on the facts presented; the Commission does
not act as a finder of fact when it provides advice (In re- Oglesby (197 5) 1 FppC Ops. 71 .)
In addition, our advice is applicable only to the extent thoJ=e facts prorrid"d to us are
correqt and that all of the material facts have been disclosed. The advice in this letter
applies only to prospective actions, as the Commission does not advise with respect to
past conduct.
QUESTTONS
1' May you participate in ad hoc subcommittee discussions, review and
substantive editing of the Draft Specific Plan, despite a conflict of interest, uelause ttre
financial effect on your economic interest is not distinguishable from its eifect on the
public generally?
' Government code sections 81000 - glo14. commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18^997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not pro,Iide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (section 83 I 14; Regulation t s:zs1c;1:;, copy enclosed).^


File No. I-07-lll
Page No. 2
2. Can segmentation be applied to portions of the ad hoc subcommittee review of
the Draft Specific Plan? In other words, may the review and editing process be broken
down into separate decisions that can be segmented from decisions in which you have a
disqualifoing financial interest?
CONCLUSIONS
1. There are insufficient facts for us to conclude whether or not your economic
interest will be affected in substantially the same manner as the public generally.
However, the facts you have provided suggest that it is unlikely that Cardiff Towne
Centre ("Towne Centre"), because of its sheer size and scope, will be affected in
substantially the same manner as the applicable significant segment.
2. You must analyze each aspect of the Draft Specific Plan decisions separately
using a reasonable and objective method to determine whether the decision is
"inextricably interrelated" to a decision that will have a material financial effect on
Towne Centre. If there are decisions that will have no financial effect -eyen a penny's
worth---on Towne Centre, then they may be "segmented," allowing you to participate
under certain circumstances as discussed below.
FACTS
You have enclosed the Draft Specific Plan, along with dozens of comments that
you wish to discuss or incorporate if you are allowed to participate in the ad hoc
committee discussion, review and substantive editing of the plan. You have included the
following relevant facts:
You are a planning commissioner representing the Cardiff-by-the-Sea area. As
discussed in our previous advice letter to you, (McCabe, supra) you have an economic
interest in your architectural business, and in Towne Centre, a shopping center that is a
client of your business, from which you have received $500 or more in income within
the past 12 months.
a
a
a
The Towne Centre is located within the boundaries of the Draft Specific Plan, which
provides a blueprint for the needs of the Cardiff-by-the-Sea business district, as
identified in the city's General Plan.
The Encinitas city council has formed an"adhoc subcommittee" to evaluate the Draft
Specific Plan. The plan addresses at the neighborhood level: land use and
development regulations; architectural, streetscape, alley design and landscape design
recommendations; vehicular and pedestrian circulation and parking; infrastructure
assessments of existing conditions for water, sewer, storm drain, and street surfaces
and po ssible improvements ; and implementation procedures.


a
File No. l-07-111
Page No. 3
The ad hoc subcommittee is composed of "12 to 13 members," who will be editing,
reviewing, and making substantive changes to the Draft Specific Plan over the next
several months.
As a planning commissioner, you represent an areathat includes 91 pieces of
commercial property within its boundaries. The Draft Specific Plan affects 66
properties, or "72.5 [percent] of the business entities in [your] jurisdiction." You also
state that in terms of business entities, or'oan individual store or business,,, the
"petcentage [affected by the Draft Specific Plan] is even greater since the specific plan
area is composed of many small businesses..."
"All properties in the specific plan area are currently zoned General Commercial.
The specific plan will become the new zoningfor all the properties at the same time
and with the same regulations."
o The Towne Centre is "a predominant element of Planning Area2" and comprises
"about 15% of the entire specific plan."
There are four contiguous planning areas in the Draft Specific Plan. Within each area are
particular zoning and use regulations that may differ from current citywide zoning
regulations. The Draft Specific Plan describes the planning areas as follows:
office use.
residential use.
Planning Area2 of the Draft Specific Plan is bound by Birmingham Drive on the
North, the alley between Newcastle Avenue and Manchester Avenue on the east,
Liverpool Drive on the south, and San Elijo Avenue on the west. It includes the Towne
Centre and the nilrow block east of the Towne Centre.
According to the Draft Specific Plan, "[t]he scale and bulk of the Towne Center is
significantly larger than other structures in the area, making it a dominant visual
feature. The Towne Centre is similar in layout to shopping centers found in older
suburban communities, with its large surface lot adjacent to the street, high visibility
from passing vehicles, and limited, though highly organized,landscaping. Buildings
are clustered at the eastern portion of the site. Sidewalks surrounding the perimeter of
the shopping center are contiguous, straight, and except for curb cuts, encompass the
entire block."
a


File No. I-07-lll
Page No. 4
o The Towne Centre is also described as "the single largest parcel" in the Draft Specific
Plan area, according to the Cardiff Community Consensus Conference report..
In your most recent letter, you contend that the ad hoc subcommittee discussions,
review, and editing of the Draft Specific Plan may be "segmented" or broken down into a
series of decisions that are separate from the decisions in which you have a conflict. you
also contend that the public generally exception should allow you to participate in most
of the editing and discussion of the Draft Specific plan.
You propose the following:
with Planning Area 2-without your participation- prior to discussions and
editing of portions dealing with the other three planning areas.
Area2-without your participation-then discussing and editing the portions of
the chapter dealing with the other planning areas.
You state that "items such as drainage, circulation, intent, community vision,
design guidelines are all general in nature and are not inextricably related."
You also wish to know whether the public generally exception is applicable in
your situation, and whether there are decisions within the ad hoc subcommittee review of
the Draft Specific Plan that may be segmented from decisions in which you are
disqualifi ed from participating in.
ANALYSIS
In our prior letter, we discussed the eight-step conflict of interest analysis. you
most recent questions concern only step 7: The public generally exception and the
question of segmentation. Accordingly, our response is limited to these two issues.
1. Public Generally
The'public generally" exception allows an official to participate in a decision
despite a conflict of interest if the effect of the decision on the official's interest is not
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. (Section 87103; Regulation
18707.) In other words, the exception would apply if the decision will affect a significant
segment of the public in substantially the same manner as it would affect the public
official's interests.


File No. I-07-l1l
Page No. 5
Regulation 18707b) sets forth a four-step process to determine "if the effect of a
decision is not distinguishable from the effect on the public generally:"
"(1) Step One: Identify each specific person or real property (economic
interest) that is materially affected by the governmental decision.
66(2) Step Two: For each person or real property identified in Step One,
determine the applicable 'significant segment' rule according to the
provisions of [regulation 1 8707. 1 (b)].
"(3) step Three: Determine if the significant segment is affected by the
governmental decision as set forth in the applicable 'significant segment,
rule. If the answer is 'no,' then the analysis ends because the first prong of
a two-part test set forth in [regulation 18707.|(b)l is not met, and the public
official cannot participate in the governmental decision. If the answer is
'yes,' proceed to Step Four.
66(4) Step Four: Following the provisions of [Regulation l87O7.lb)e)],
determine if the person or real property identified in step one is affected
by the govemmental decision in osubstantially the same manner' as other
persons or real property in the applicable significant segment. If the
answer is 'yes' as to each person or real property identified in Step one,
then the effect of the decision is not distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally and the public official may participate in the decision. If
the answer is 'no' as to any person or real property identified in Step one,
the public official may not participate in the governmental decision unless
one of the special rules set forth in [regulations 18707.2 through ls7o7.g)
applies to each person or real property triggering the conflict of interest.,,
(Regulation I 8707(bX I )-(4).)
Step One: Your conflict of interest in the Cardiff Towne Center was addressed in
our previous advice letter to yol, (McCabe, supra.) As concluded in that letter to you, it
is presumed that governmental decisions involved in the Draft Specific Plan review will
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Towne centre.
Step Two: Source of Income -Business Entities (Towne Centre)3 -The applicable
"significant segment" for decisions that affect an interest in a business entity, under
regulation 18707.1(b)(1XC), is "either 2,000 or twenty-five percent of all business
entities in the jurisdiction or the district the official represents, so long as the effect is on
persons composed of more than a single industry, trade, or profession."
3 In ourprevious advice letter to you, we also identified your architectural business as a source of
income, but because you have not provided additional information related to the public generally exception
with regard to this economic interest, we do not include it in our analysis.


File No. I-07-111
Page No. 6
Step Three.' Business Entities (Towne Centre) - You have indicatedthat 66
commercial properties (out of 91) or 72.5 percent of commercial properties will be
affected by decisions involving the Draft Specific Plan. You also state that the
percentage of business entities affected "is even greater." If the specific governmental
decisions pertaining to discussions, review, and editing of the Draft Specific Plan will
affect at least twenty-five percent of the business entities, as provided in Regulation
18707.1b)(1XC), then this requirement has been met.
Step Four: The final step requires a determination that the financial effects of the
decisions in question impact your economic interests in "substantially the same manner,,
as the financial effects on the significant segment identified in Step 3. If the answer is
"yes," then the effect of the decision is not distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally and you may participate in the decision. (Reg. 18707b)$).) To assess
whether a significant segment will be financially affected in substantially the same
manner, all measurable effects from the decision must be identified. Comparing financial
effects is necessarily afactual process.
Your economic interest is in Towne Centre, a business entity that rents or leases
commercial property. Therefore, it is relevant to consider the financial effects of the
decisions on the real property owned by this business entity, as well as any financial
effects on the business itself.* The materiality standard for business entities includes
financial effects of a governmental decision on its assets. (Regulation 18705.1.)
Usually, a specific plan does not affect all properties or business entities within
the specific plan in the same manner. (See Dietrtck Advice Letter, No. I-02-325.)
For instance, a specific plan could affect streets immediately adjacent to an official's
property or business, while not extending to properties or businesses at another location
within the specific plan. Thus, specific plan decisions could significantly impact the
value of an official's property or business interest, while not affecting other properties or
businesses in the specific plan area. Therefore, an official could not invoke thei'public
generally'' exception.
Your facts indicate that Towne Centre is an unusually large parcel in the plan
area, and it could be affected differently because of its type of business. These are just
examples of factors that may be considered and the kinds of effects that would notbe
considered "substantially the same."
o In the past, we have advised that many factors play a role in determining whether or not
economic interests in real property will be financially affected in a manner substantially the same as the
significant segment identified including, but not limited to: the proximity of an individual, property, or
business to a specific project or property subject to the government decision; location ofa proferty or
business; impact on an individual, property, or business from noise or traflic; impact on u b,sir"s, o.
property due to development potential; and the size of a property or business. (Sie Berger Advice Letter,
No. 4-05-054; also see Regulation 18707.1(b)(2XA).)


File No. I-07-l1l
Page No. 7
You state in your July 16, 2007 letter that "All properties in the specific plan area
are currently zoned. General Commercial" and that the specific plan "will become the
new zoning for all the properties at the same time and with the same regulations." You
conclude that therefore "a11 properties are affected in the substantially the same manner."
Although you state that specific plan zoningrequirements will apply to all
properties in the specific plan area, ardthat other business entities will be affected by the
governmental decisions, you do not include information about specific financial effects of
the Draft Specific Plan decisions on Towne Centre, or any other affected business entity.
In addition, you state that Towne Centre-which is the single largest parcel in the
planning area-comprises "about 15% of the entire specific plan" and occupies an "entire
block."
Because you have not provided sufficient facts, we are unable to determine
whether your economic interest will be affected in substantially the same manner as the
public generally. However, the facts you have provided suggest that it is unlikely that
Towne Centre, because of its sheer size and scope, will be affected in substantially the
same manner as the applicable significant segment.
2. Segmentation:
We have advised that certain large and complex decisions may, under certain
circumstances, be divided into separate decisions so that an official who has a
disqualifying interest in one component of the decision may still participate in other
components in which the official has no financial interest. (MerkuloffAdvice Letter, No.
I-90-542; Huffaker Advice Letter, No. 4-86-343.) However, in some cases, decisions are
too "inextricably related" to be considered separately and, in such cases, a public
official's conflict of interest in one decision will disqualify him or her from participating
in the other decisions.
As discussed in our previous advice letter to you, decisions are "inextricably
intetelated " where, among other things, one decision is a necessary condition precedent
or condition subsequent for another. (See, for example, Stone Advice Letter,No. 4-06-
007; Ball Advice Letter, No. A-98-124.) Decisions are also considered inextricably
interrelated when the result of one decision will effectively determine, ffirm, nullify, or
alterthe result of another decision. (Regulation 18709(b).)
Similar to the public generally analysis, segmentation can be analyzed only after
certain preliminary steps are taken. First, all those decisions that will have a material
financial effect on your economic interest must be identified and separated from those
that would not have such an effect. Because your economic interest is directly involved,
even a penny's effect would be considered material. Thus, each of those decisions that


File No. l-07-l1l
Page No. 8
will affect your economic interest by even one penny, are must be segmented from those
decisions that will not have a material effect on your source of income. 
5
You propose that discussions and editing of the portion of the Draft Specific plan
dealing with Planning Area 2 (which includes Towne Centre), may be done first, without
your participation, prior to the other three planning areas. This includes portions of
Chapter 3, which deals with land use and development regulations pertaining to planning
Area2. You also suggest that the decisions involving other planning areas, and general
guidelines affecting the entire Draft Specific Plan-such as drainage, circulation, intent,
community vision, design guidelines-are all general in nature and maybe segmented
from decisions in which you have a disqualifying financial interest because these
decisions will have "no financial effect," on Towne Centre.
However, grven that Towne Centre is such alarge part of the specific plan, it is
substantially likely that the business and its commercial property will experience at least
one penny's effect with regard to virtually all the revisions to the Draft Specific plan.
Indeed, upon review of the Draft Specific Plan, it appears that there numerous decisions
within each chapter applying general guidelines that will also have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial impact on Towne Centre. Accordingly, it appears that the
decisions are "inextricably interrelated" and, therefore, may not be segmenied.
In the past, we have advised that specific plan decisions that designate specific
land uses-such as the planned type, intensity, and location of land uses-will almost
inevitably have some impact on the value of property within the plan area. (McCain
Advice Letter,I-06-031; Busby Advice Letter, No. .4.-05-159.) So, for example, the
Draft Specific Plan recommendations involving land use and development regulations-
such as allowing retail outlets and offices and residences on upper floors in most of the
area between San Elijo and Newcastle south of Birmingham Drive (which is classified as
"commercial mixed l"Fwould likely have a one-penny effect on the Towne Centre
property and its commercial property rental/lease business.
Likewise, decisions that concern public street, landscape and sidewalk
improvements,6 new or improved public service infrastructure involving water, sewer,
s Please note that our advice is limited to the facts presented. (Section 831 14.) Ultimately, the
public official with the potential conflict of interest bears the responsibility of deciding whether a material
financial effect is reasonably foreseeable. Evidence that there will be no financial "ff""t 
o., real property
may take the form of an independent appraisal. Such an appraisal may be appropriate evidence on which to
rely when determining whether there will be no financial effect on real properry or business interests.
However, reliance on an appraisal immunizes the official only to the extent that such reliance is reasonable
at the time ofthe decision. The actual sufficiency ofany appraisal, is ofcourse, a question offact that the
Commission cannot decide. (In re Oglesby (1975) I FppC Ops. 71.)
u These are referenced in Chapter 4: Design Recommendations.


File No. I-07-lll
Page No. 9
storm drains,T or improved vehicular, pedestrian and bicycle access,s also appear quite
likely to enhance the value of properties and businesses in the area.
We have also advised that renovation of a downtown area would likely lead to an
increase in the value ofproperties in the vicinity. (Siegel Advice Letter, No. i-eO-OSZ.;
It would thus be reasonably foreseeable that Towne Centre (which consists of
most of the land area in Planning Area2), may experience a financial effect from
policies or guidelines that are intended to enhance the properties that are in proximity to
Planning Area 1 and 3, or even 4, for instance.
On the other hand, such things as the size of window signs, the elimination of free
standing A-frame signs, or the width of sidewhlks and other decisions affecting properties
of some distance from Planning Area 2,may not have afrnancial effect on Towne Centre.
Therefore, you must analyze each aspect of the Draft Specific Plan decisions
separately using a reasonable and objective method to determine whether the decision
will have a material financial effect on Towne Centre.
If any of the decisions are not "inextricably interrelated" they may be considered
separately, and the following procedure should be followed:
The decisions for which you have a disqualifying financial interest
must be segregated from the other decisions;
The decisions for which you are disqualified must be considered first,
and a final decision reached without your participation;
once a decision has been made on the portions of the Draft specific
Plan in which you have a disqualiffing interest, you mayparticipate in
deliberations regarding other portions of the plan, so longas thoie
deliberations do not result in the reopening or in any way affect the
decisions from which you are disqualified. (Huffaker Letter, supra;
Also see Regulation 18709.)
Based on the above factors, the decisions in which you have afrnancialdecision
must be considered first-such as those pertaining to Planning Area2, as well as portions
of each chapter that would have a financial effect on Towne Centre-and a final decision
must be reached before the other decisions are made.
1
2.
J
7 These are referenced in Chapter 6: Infrastructure.
8 These are included in Chapter 5: Circulation and parking.


File No. I-07-lll
Page No. 10
If you have any further questions regarding this matter, please feel free to contact
me at 322-5660
Sincerely
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Emelyn
Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosure
ERjgl

inevitably have some impact on the value of property within the plan area. (McCain
Advice Letter,I-06-031; Busby Advice Letter, No. .4.-05-159.) So, for example, the
Draft Specific Plan recommendations involving land use and development regulations-
such as allowing retail outlets and offices and residences on upper floors in most of the
area between San Elijo and Newcastle south of Birmingham Drive (which is classified as
"commercial mixed l"Fwould likely have a one-penny effect on the Towne Centre
property and its commercial property rental/lease business.
Likewise, decisions that concern public street, landscape and sidewalk
improvements,6 new or improved public service infrastructure involving water, sewer,
s Please note that our advice is limited to the facts presented. (Section 831 14.) Ultimately, the
public official with the potential conflict of interest bears the responsibility of deciding whether a material
financial effect is reasonably foreseeable. Evidence that there will be no financial "ff""t 
o., real property
may take the form of an independent appraisal. Such an appraisal may be appropriate evidence on which to
rely when determining whether there will be no financial effect on real properry or business interests.
However, reliance on an appraisal immunizes the official only to the extent that such reliance is reasonable
at the time ofthe decision. The actual sufficiency ofany appraisal, is ofcourse, a question offact that the
Commission cannot decide. (In re Oglesby (1975) I FppC Ops. 71.)
u These are referenced in Chapter 4: Design Recommendations.


File No. I-07-lll
storm drains,T or improved vehicular, pedestrian and bicycle access,s also appear quite
likely to enhance the value of properties and businesses in the area.
We have also advised that renovation of a downtown area would likely lead to an
increase in the value ofproperties in the vicinity. (Siegel Advice Letter, No. i-eO-OSZ.;
It would thus be reasonably foreseeable that Towne Centre (which consists of
most of the land area in Planning Area2), may experience a financial effect from
policies or guidelines that are intended to enhance the properties that are in proximity to
Planning Area 1 and 3, or even 4, for instance.
On the other hand, such things as the size of window signs, the elimination of free
standing A-frame signs, or the width of sidewhlks and other decisions affecting properties
of some distance from Planning Area 2,may not have afrnancial effect on Towne Centre.
Therefore, you must analyze each aspect of the Draft Specific Plan decisions
separately using a reasonable and objective method to determine whether the decision
will have a material financial effect on Towne Centre.
If any of the decisions are not "inextricably interrelated" they may be considered
separately, and the following procedure should be followed:
The decisions for which you have a disqualifying financial interest
must be segregated from the other decisions;
The decisions for which you are disqualified must be considered first,
and a final decision reached without your participation;
once a decision has been made on the portions of the Draft specific
Plan in which you have a disqualiffing interest, you mayparticipate in
deliberations regarding other portions of the plan, so longas thoie
deliberations do not result in the reopening or in any way affect the
decisions from which you are disqualified. (Huffaker Letter, supra;
Also see Regulation 18709.)
Based on the above factors, the decisions in which you have afrnancialdecision
must be considered first-such as those pertaining to Planning Area2, as well as portions
of each chapter that would have a financial effect on Towne Centre-and a final decision
must be reached before the other decisions are made.

1. There are insufficient facts for us to conclude whether or not your economic
interest will be affected in substantially the same manner as the public generally.
However, the facts you have provided suggest that it is unlikely that Cardiff Towne
Centre ("Towne Centre"), because of its sheer size and scope, will be affected in
substantially the same manner as the applicable significant segment.
2. You must analyze each aspect of the Draft Specific Plan decisions separately
using a reasonable and objective method to determine whether the decision is
"inextricably interrelated" to a decision that will have a material financial effect on
Towne Centre. If there are decisions that will have no financial effect -eyen a penny's
worth---on Towne Centre, then they may be "segmented," allowing you to participate

sections.facts:
You have enclosed the Draft Specific Plan, along with dozens of comments that
you wish to discuss or incorporate if you are allowed to participate in the ad hoc
committee discussion, review and substantive editing of the plan. You have included the
following relevant facts:
You are a planning commissioner representing the Cardiff-by-the-Sea area. As
discussed in our previous advice letter to you, (McCabe, supra) you have an economic
interest in your architectural business, and in Towne Centre, a shopping center that is a
client of your business, from which you have received $500 or more in income within
the past 12 months.
a
a
a
The Towne Centre is located within the boundaries of the Draft Specific Plan, which
provides a blueprint for the needs of the Cardiff-by-the-Sea business district, as
identified in the city's General Plan.
The Encinitas city council has formed an"adhoc subcommittee" to evaluate the Draft
Specific Plan. The plan addresses at the neighborhood level: land use and
development regulations; architectural, streetscape, alley design and landscape design
recommendations; vehicular and pedestrian circulation and parking; infrastructure
assessments of existing conditions for water, sewer, storm drain, and street surfaces
and po ssible improvements ; and implementation procedures.


a
File No. l-07-111
The ad hoc subcommittee is composed of "12 to 13 members," who will be editing,
reviewing, and making substantive changes to the Draft Specific Plan over the next
several months.
As a planning commissioner, you represent an areathat includes 91 pieces of
commercial property within its boundaries. The Draft Specific Plan affects 66
properties, or "72.5 [percent] of the business entities in [your] jurisdiction." You also
state that in terms of business entities, or'oan individual store or business,,, the
"petcentage [affected by the Draft Specific Plan] is even greater since the specific plan
area is composed of many small businesses..."
"All properties in the specific plan area are currently zoned General Commercial.
The specific plan will become the new zoningfor all the properties at the same time
and with the same regulations."
o The Towne Centre is "a predominant element of Planning Area2" and comprises
"about 15% of the entire specific plan."
There are four contiguous planning areas in the Draft Specific Plan. Within each area are
particular zoning and use regulations that may differ from current citywide zoning
regulations. The Draft Specific Plan describes the planning areas as follows:
office use.
residential use.
Planning Area2 of the Draft Specific Plan is bound by Birmingham Drive on the
North, the alley between Newcastle Avenue and Manchester Avenue on the east,
Liverpool Drive on the south, and San Elijo Avenue on the west. It includes the Towne
Centre and the nilrow block east of the Towne Centre.
According to the Draft Specific Plan, "[t]he scale and bulk of the Towne Center is
significantly larger than other structures in the area, making it a dominant visual
feature. The Towne Centre is similar in layout to shopping centers found in older
suburban communities, with its large surface lot adjacent to the street, high visibility
from passing vehicles, and limited, though highly organized,landscaping. Buildings
are clustered at the eastern portion of the site. Sidewalks surrounding the perimeter of
the shopping center are contiguous, straight, and except for curb cuts, encompass the
entire block."
a


File No. I-07-lll
o The Towne Centre is also described as "the single largest parcel" in the Draft Specific
Plan area, according to the Cardiff Community Consensus Conference report..
In your most recent letter, you contend that the ad hoc subcommittee discussions,
review, and editing of the Draft Specific Plan may be "segmented" or broken down into a
series of decisions that are separate from the decisions in which you have a conflict. you
also contend that the public generally exception should allow you to participate in most
of the editing and discussion of the Draft Specific plan.
You propose the following:
with Planning Area 2-without your participation- prior to discussions and
editing of portions dealing with the other three planning areas.
Area2-without your participation-then discussing and editing the portions of
the chapter dealing with the other planning areas.
You state that "items such as drainage, circulation, intent, community vision,
design guidelines are all general in nature and are not inextricably related."
You also wish to know whether the public generally exception is applicable in
your situation, and whether there are decisions within the ad hoc subcommittee review of
the Draft Specific Plan that may be segmented from decisions in which you are
disqualifi ed from participating in.
sections.analysis:
In our prior letter, we discussed the eight-step conflict of interest analysis. you
most recent questions concern only step 7: The public generally exception and the

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: of segmentation. Accordingly, our response is limited to these two issues.
1. Public Generally
The'public generally" exception allows an official to participate in a decision
despite a conflict of interest if the effect of the decision on the official's interest is not
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. (Section 87103; Regulation
18707.) In other words, the exception would apply if the decision will affect a significant
segment of the public in substantially the same manner as it would affect the public
official's interests.


File No. I-07-l1l
Regulation 18707b) sets forth a four-step process to determine "if the effect of a
decision is not distinguishable from the effect on the public generally:"
"(1) Step One: Identify each specific person or real property (economic
interest) that is materially affected by the governmental decision.
66(2) Step Two: For each person or real property identified in Step One,
determine the applicable 'significant segment' rule according to the
provisions of [regulation 1 8707. 1 (b)].
"(3) step Three: Determine if the significant segment is affected by the
governmental decision as set forth in the applicable 'significant segment,
rule. If the answer is 'no,' then the analysis ends because the first prong of
a two-part test set forth in [regulation 18707.|(b)l is not met, and the public
official cannot participate in the governmental decision. If the answer is
'yes,' proceed to Step Four.
66(4) Step Four: Following the provisions of [Regulation l87O7.lb)e)],
determine if the person or real property identified in step one is affected
by the govemmental decision in osubstantially the same manner' as other
persons or real property in the applicable significant segment. If the
answer is 'yes' as to each person or real property identified in Step one,
then the effect of the decision is not distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally and the public official may participate in the decision. If
the answer is 'no' as to any person or real property identified in Step one,
the public official may not participate in the governmental decision unless
one of the special rules set forth in [regulations 18707.2 through ls7o7.g)
applies to each person or real property triggering the conflict of interest.,,
(Regulation I 8707(bX I )-(4).)
Step One: Your conflict of interest in the Cardiff Towne Center was addressed in
our previous advice letter to yol, (McCabe, supra.) As concluded in that letter to you, it
is presumed that governmental decisions involved in the Draft Specific Plan review will
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Towne centre.
Step Two: Source of Income -Business Entities (Towne Centre)3 -The applicable
"significant segment" for decisions that affect an interest in a business entity, under
regulation 18707.1(b)(1XC), is "either 2,000 or twenty-five percent of all business
entities in the jurisdiction or the district the official represents, so long as the effect is on
persons composed of more than a single industry, trade, or profession."
3 In ourprevious advice letter to you, we also identified your architectural business as a source of
income, but because you have not provided additional information related to the public generally exception
with regard to this economic interest, we do not include it in our analysis.
Step Three.' Business Entities (Towne Centre) - You have indicatedthat 66
commercial properties (out of 91) or 72.5 percent of commercial properties will be
affected by decisions involving the Draft Specific Plan. You also state that the
percentage of business entities affected "is even greater." If the specific governmental
decisions pertaining to discussions, review, and editing of the Draft Specific Plan will
affect at least twenty-five percent of the business entities, as provided in Regulation
18707.1b)(1XC), then this requirement has been met.
Step Four: The final step requires a determination that the financial effects of the
decisions in question impact your economic interests in "substantially the same manner,,
as the financial effects on the significant segment identified in Step 3. If the answer is
"yes," then the effect of the decision is not distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally and you may participate in the decision. (Reg. 18707b)$).) To assess
whether a significant segment will be financially affected in substantially the same
manner, all measurable effects from the decision must be identified. Comparing financial
effects is necessarily afactual process.
Your economic interest is in Towne Centre, a business entity that rents or leases
commercial property. Therefore, it is relevant to consider the financial effects of the
decisions on the real property owned by this business entity, as well as any financial
effects on the business itself.* The materiality standard for business entities includes
financial effects of a governmental decision on its assets. (Regulation 18705.1.)
Usually, a specific plan does not affect all properties or business entities within
the specific plan in the same manner. (See Dietrtck Advice Letter, No. I-02-325.)
For instance, a specific plan could affect streets immediately adjacent to an official's
property or business, while not extending to properties or businesses at another location
within the specific plan. Thus, specific plan decisions could significantly impact the
value of an official's property or business interest, while not affecting other properties or
businesses in the specific plan area. Therefore, an official could not invoke thei'public
generally'' exception.
Your facts indicate that Towne Centre is an unusually large parcel in the plan
area, and it could be affected differently because of its type of business. These are just
examples of factors that may be considered and the kinds of effects that would notbe
considered "substantially the same."
o In the past, we have advised that many factors play a role in determining whether or not
economic interests in real property will be financially affected in a manner substantially the same as the
significant segment identified including, but not limited to: the proximity of an individual, property, or
business to a specific project or property subject to the government decision; location ofa proferty or
business; impact on an individual, property, or business from noise or traflic; impact on u b,sir"s, o.
property due to development potential; and the size of a property or business. (Sie Berger Advice Letter,
No. 4-05-054; also see Regulation 18707.1(b)(2XA).)


File No. I-07-l1l
You state in your July 16, 2007 letter that "All properties in the specific plan area
are currently zoned. General Commercial" and that the specific plan "will become the
new zoning for all the properties at the same time and with the same regulations." You
conclude that therefore "a11 properties are affected in the substantially the same manner."
Although you state that specific plan zoningrequirements will apply to all
properties in the specific plan area, ardthat other business entities will be affected by the
governmental decisions, you do not include information about specific financial effects of
the Draft Specific Plan decisions on Towne Centre, or any other affected business entity.
In addition, you state that Towne Centre-which is the single largest parcel in the
planning area-comprises "about 15% of the entire specific plan" and occupies an "entire
block."
Because you have not provided sufficient facts, we are unable to determine
whether your economic interest will be affected in substantially the same manner as the
public generally. However, the facts you have provided suggest that it is unlikely that
Towne Centre, because of its sheer size and scope, will be affected in substantially the
same manner as the applicable significant segment.
2. Segmentation:
We have advised that certain large and complex decisions may, under certain
circumstances, be divided into separate decisions so that an official who has a
disqualifying interest in one component of the decision may still participate in other
components in which the official has no financial interest. (MerkuloffAdvice Letter, No.
I-90-542; Huffaker Advice Letter, No. 4-86-343.) However, in some cases, decisions are
too "inextricably related" to be considered separately and, in such cases, a public
official's conflict of interest in one decision will disqualify him or her from participating
in the other decisions.
As discussed in our previous advice letter to you, decisions are "inextricably
intetelated " where, among other things, one decision is a necessary condition precedent
or condition subsequent for another. (See, for example, Stone Advice Letter,No. 4-06-
007; Ball Advice Letter, No. A-98-124.) Decisions are also considered inextricably
interrelated when the result of one decision will effectively determine, ffirm, nullify, or
alterthe result of another decision. (Regulation 18709(b).)
Similar to the public generally analysis, segmentation can be analyzed only after
certain preliminary steps are taken. First, all those decisions that will have a material
financial effect on your economic interest must be identified and separated from those
that would not have such an effect. Because your economic interest is directly involved,
even a penny's effect would be considered material. Thus, each of those decisions that


File No. l-07-l1l
will affect your economic interest by even one penny, are must be segmented from those
decisions that will not have a material effect on your source of income. 
5
You propose that discussions and editing of the portion of the Draft Specific plan
dealing with Planning Area 2 (which includes Towne Centre), may be done first, without
your participation, prior to the other three planning areas. This includes portions of
Chapter 3, which deals with land use and development regulations pertaining to planning
Area2. You also suggest that the decisions involving other planning areas, and general
guidelines affecting the entire Draft Specific Plan-such as drainage, circulation, intent,
community vision, design guidelines-are all general in nature and maybe segmented
from decisions in which you have a disqualifying financial interest because these
decisions will have "no financial effect," on Towne Centre.
However, grven that Towne Centre is such alarge part of the specific plan, it is
substantially likely that the business and its commercial property will experience at least
one penny's effect with regard to virtually all the revisions to the Draft Specific plan.
Indeed, upon review of the Draft Specific Plan, it appears that there numerous decisions
within each chapter applying general guidelines that will also have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial impact on Towne Centre. Accordingly, it appears that the
decisions are "inextricably interrelated" and, therefore, may not be segmenied.
In the past, we have advised that specific plan decisions that designate specific
land uses-such as the planned type, intensity, and location of land uses-will almost
inevitably have some impact on the value of property within the plan area. (McCain
Advice Letter,I-06-031; Busby Advice Letter, No. .4.-05-159.) So, for example, the
Draft Specific Plan recommendations involving land use and development regulations-
such as allowing retail outlets and offices and residences on upper floors in most of the
area between San Elijo and Newcastle south of Birmingham Drive (which is classified as
"commercial mixed l"Fwould likely have a one-penny effect on the Towne Centre
property and its commercial property rental/lease business.
Likewise, decisions that concern public street, landscape and sidewalk
improvements,6 new or improved public service infrastructure involving water, sewer,
s Please note that our advice is limited to the facts presented. (Section 831 14.) Ultimately, the
public official with the potential conflict of interest bears the responsibility of deciding whether a material
financial effect is reasonably foreseeable. Evidence that there will be no financial "ff""t 
o., real property
may take the form of an independent appraisal. Such an appraisal may be appropriate evidence on which to
rely when determining whether there will be no financial effect on real properry or business interests.
However, reliance on an appraisal immunizes the official only to the extent that such reliance is reasonable
at the time ofthe decision. The actual sufficiency ofany appraisal, is ofcourse, a question offact that the
Commission cannot decide. (In re Oglesby (1975) I FppC Ops. 71.)
u These are referenced in Chapter 4: Design Recommendations.


File No. I-07-lll
storm drains,T or improved vehicular, pedestrian and bicycle access,s also appear quite
likely to enhance the value of properties and businesses in the area.
We have also advised that renovation of a downtown area would likely lead to an
increase in the value ofproperties in the vicinity. (Siegel Advice Letter, No. i-eO-OSZ.;
It would thus be reasonably foreseeable that Towne Centre (which consists of
most of the land area in Planning Area2), may experience a financial effect from
policies or guidelines that are intended to enhance the properties that are in proximity to
Planning Area 1 and 3, or even 4, for instance.
On the other hand, such things as the size of window signs, the elimination of free
standing A-frame signs, or the width of sidewhlks and other decisions affecting properties
of some distance from Planning Area 2,may not have afrnancial effect on Towne Centre.
Therefore, you must analyze each aspect of the Draft Specific Plan decisions
separately using a reasonable and objective method to determine whether the decision
will have a material financial effect on Towne Centre.
If any of the decisions are not "inextricably interrelated" they may be considered
separately, and the following procedure should be followed:
The decisions for which you have a disqualifying financial interest
must be segregated from the other decisions;
The decisions for which you are disqualified must be considered first,
and a final decision reached without your participation;
once a decision has been made on the portions of the Draft specific
Plan in which you have a disqualiffing interest, you mayparticipate in
deliberations regarding other portions of the plan, so longas thoie
deliberations do not result in the reopening or in any way affect the
decisions from which you are disqualified. (Huffaker Letter, supra;
Also see Regulation 18709.)
Based on the above factors, the decisions in which you have afrnancialdecision
must be considered first-such as those pertaining to Planning Area2, as well as portions
of each chapter that would have a financial effect on Towne Centre-and a final decision
must be reached before the other decisions are made.
1
2.
J
7 These are referenced in Chapter 6: Infrastructure.
8 These are included in Chapter 5: Circulation and parking.


File No. I-07-lll

CONCLUSION: 1. There are insufficient facts for us to conclude whether or not your economic
interest will be affected in substantially the same manner as the public generally.
However, the facts you have provided suggest that it is unlikely that Cardiff Towne
Centre ("Towne Centre"), because of its sheer size and scope, will be affected in
substantially the same manner as the applicable significant segment.
2. You must analyze each aspect of the Draft Specific Plan decisions separately
using a reasonable and objective method to determine whether the decision is
"inextricably interrelated" to a decision that will have a material financial effect on
Towne Centre. If there are decisions that will have no financial effect -eyen a penny's
worth---on Towne Centre, then they may be "segmented," allowing you to participate
under certain circumstances as discussed below.
